text: The surgeon begins the dissection of the mediastinal pleura around the pulmonary hilum using electrocautery or a harmonic scalpel. Despite the presence of inflammatory tissue, the adhesions surrounding the hilum are easily dissected.

The surgeon uses a hook instrument to dissect towards the back wall of the pulmonary hilum, aiming for precise identification of anatomical structures while minimizing bleeding. The back wall of the airway becomes visible during this step.

The surgeon identifies a lymph node located between the anterior truncus and the superior pulmonary vein. The anterior truncus is well dissected, and the artery is prepared for transection using a vascular stapler.

Through the single port access, the surgeon dissects the bronchus, focusing on the airway to achieve a good release of the distal trachea, right upper lobe bronchus, and bronchus intermediate. The back part of the airway is carefully dissected to facilitate mobilization for anastomosis.

During the dissection, the surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy, noting the absence of lymph nodes at station seven due to a previous mediastinoscopy. A lymph node at station eleven, located at the level of the sump, is identified and sent for analysis.

The surgeon returns to the anterior view to dissect the superior pulmonary vein, clearly identifying the middle lobe branch and the right upper lobe branch. The dissection proceeds towards the pericardium, revealing the plane between the superior pulmonary vein and the pulmonary artery.

The surgeon develops the plane between the superior pulmonary vein and the pulmonary artery, allowing safe control of the right upper lobe plane. The insertion of the pericardium is visible, indicating the area for dissection.

The surgeon focuses on dissecting the plane between the right upper lobe structures, carefully separating the superior pulmonary vein from the main pulmonary artery to minimize vascular accidents and bleeding.
The surgeon carefully pulls the superior pulmonary vein anteriorly, minimizing manipulation of the pulmonary artery while releasing the vein. The vein is prepared for surrounding.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the pericardium, carefully avoiding manipulation of the pulmonary artery located behind the superior pulmonary vein. The vein is surrounded safely, maintaining a clear separation from the artery.
The surgeon encounters a small amount of tissue while performing the takedown of the superior lobe vein during a right upper lobectomy using a single port approach.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the superior pulmonary vein, facilitating its mobilization for stapling. The staple is passed through the vein without tension, demonstrating a smooth and uncomplicated procedure.

The surgeon has dissected and controlled the superior pulmonary vein and is now proceeding towards the airway to release the main pulmonary artery. No other vascular structures are visible in the region, suggesting the absence of a superior ascending branch.

The surgeon completes the fissure between the right upper lobe and the middle lobe, revealing the right upper lobe vein stump and the main pulmonary artery. The space above the bronchus intermedius is visible during this process.
The surgeon opens the proximal part of the airway, revealing the endobronchial lesion located inside the right upper lobe bronchus, with no visible infiltration into the main airway.
The surgical field appears clean, and the surgeon uses a scalpel to make a small incision on the opposite side for better control, revealing healthy tissue.
The surgeon completes the back wall of the bronchial anastomosis, addressing a previous mismatch in the cut. The suture technique involves using a single line with both needles retained.

The surgeon uses two needles to create a continuous suture line, with one needle moving forward and the other backward until they meet. The surgeon holds the suture line under tension with one hand while placing stitches with the other, providing support for precise stitch placement.

Of course, some of you could argue that my left hand should be using another instrument to pull the needle, but I find it easier to just hold the stitch to bring it towards me and that will give me a little bit of traction that I need to place well my stitches. It's important also to have a kind of very light needle holder. just easier because this is a delicate suture, delicate anastomosis.

The surgeon continues placing stitches for the anastomosis, balancing the suture placement between the anterior and posterior parts to ensure ease of access and avoid complications. The anterior section is partially completed, and the focus shifts to the posterior section, maintaining equilibrium between both ends.
It's not that difficult. The other thing is, if I hold the stitch with my left hand, I probably decrease the number of cases that my line of suture gets stuck or crossed over so we can see better.
This video hasn't been that much edited. We were really able to put both ends nice and easy together. So now we're probably going back to the anterior stitch, the anterior line of suture.

The surgeon applies a balanced level of traction to maintain the straightness of the needle while completing the anastomosis. The anterior and posterior lines of suture are crossed to finalize the connection.
The surgeon ties both ends of the suture together, ensuring the anastomosis is secure. A pleural flap is placed around the anastomosis to prevent the pulmonary artery from rubbing against it.
The surgeon places two stitches to secure the intercostal nerve block, visible near the chest tip.
